skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
skill.summary: A meta-skill that teaches Claude Code to always invoke relevant skills before responding, ensuring proper workflow discipline
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: All four static findings are false positives. The detected patterns (Ruby/shell backticks and weak cryptographic algorithms) are Markdown formatting syntax (backticks for code spans and code blocks) and do not represent actual security risks. This is a pure documentation skill containing workflow guidance.

medium_findings.title: False Positive: External Commands Pattern
medium_findings.description: Scanner detected Ruby/shell backtick execution at SKILL.md:16 and SKILL.md:26, but these are Markdown backticks for code formatting (the Skill tool name and DOT diagram syntax), not actual shell commands. This is a documentation file with no executable code. Confidence: 0.95 - The backticks are Markdown syntax for code formatting, not shell execution operators.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm
low_findings.description: Scanner flagged lines 3 and 72 as containing weak cryptographic algorithms. This appears to be pattern matching confusion - line 3 contains YAML frontmatter description, line 72 contains Implementation skills. No cryptographic operations exist in this file. Confidence: 0.98 - The scanner appears to have confused text patterns.

content.value_statement: This meta-skill establishes critical workflow discipline by requiring Claude Code to check for and invoke relevant skills before any response or action, preventing missed opportunities and rationalization.
actual_capabilities: Instructs Claude Code to invoke the Skill tool before ANY response
Provides a decision flowchart for when to check for skills
Lists common rationalization patterns that lead to skipping skill checks
Defines skill priority order (process skills before implementation skills)
Distinguishes between rigid skills (must follow exactly) and flexible skills (adapt principles)
Prevents premature actions by enforcing skill check discipline
limitations: This is a meta-skill that guides behavior, not a task-performing skill
It does not provide specific technical capabilities
The skill must be invoked at the start of conversation to be effective
Relies on user conversation context to determine skill relevance

use_cases.title: New Conversation Starter
use_cases.description: At the start of any new conversation, invoke this skill to establish workflow discipline before any task is attempted
use_cases.target_user: Any Claude Code user starting a new task

use_cases.title: Skill Verification Helper
use_cases.description: Use when unsure whether a skill applies to your current task - this skill enforces checking rather than assuming

use_cases.title: Workflow Refresher
use_cases.description: Invoke periodically during long conversations to re-establish discipline if workflow slips
use_cases.target_user: Users working on complex multi-step tasks

prompt_templates.title: Basic Skill Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I invoke any skills before responding to: [describe your task]?
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new task or receiving user request

prompt_templates.title: Skill Priority Check
prompt_templates.prompt: What skills might apply to this task? List them in priority order based on the using-superpowers skill.

prompt_templates.title: Rationalization Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: Am I rationalizing my way out of checking for skills? Check against these red flags from using-superpowers: [list from skill]
prompt_templates.scenario: When tempted to skip skill invocation

prompt_templates.title: Complex Task Skill Planning
prompt_templates.prompt: For this complex task, identify: 1) Process skills to determine approach, 2) Implementation skills to guide execution. Order matters per using-superpowers.

output_examples.input: User asks: Help me write a Python script
output: I notice you want me to write code. First, let me check for relevant skills using the using-superpowers approach. I'll invoke the Skill tool to find coding-related skills before proceeding.

output_examples.input: User asks: Debug this error in my React app
output: Before diving into debugging, I should check for skills. Per the using-superpowers workflow, I'll invoke the Skill tool to see if debugging or React-specific skills apply.

output_examples.input: User says: Let me explore the codebase first
output: Per using-superpowers, that thought is a red flag - 'Let me explore the codebase first' means I'm rationalizing. Skills tell me HOW to explore. Let me check for relevant skills first.

best_practices: Invoke this skill at the VERY start of every conversation before any response
Treat even 1% chance of skill applicability as a reason to invoke the Skill tool
Read skill content fresh each time - skills evolve over time
Never rationalize your way out of checking for skills - use the red flags list
anti_patterns: Responding to simple questions without checking for relevant skills first
Exploring a codebase before checking what skills tell you about exploration
Assuming you remember a skill and skipping the actual invocation
Deciding a task is too simple to warrant skill invocation

faq.question: Does this skill work with all Claude Code versions?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a documentation skill that provides workflow guidance. It teaches the principle of skill invocation that applies to all Claude Code versions.

faq.question: Do I need to invoke this skill manually every time?
faq.answer: Yes, this meta-skill must be invoked at conversation start. It establishes the discipline to check for other skills before any response.

faq.question: What if a skill is not relevant to my task?
faq.answer: If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you do not need to use it. The skill check is still valuable discipline.

faq.question: How is this different from regular skills?
faq.answer: This is a meta-skill that guides workflow behavior. Unlike task-specific skills, it does not perform actions but teaches proper skill invocation discipline.

faq.question: Can this skill conflict with other skills?
faq.answer: No, it works alongside other skills. Process skills (like brainstorming) should be invoked first, then implementation skills, per the guidance in this skill.

faq.question: What if I forget to invoke this skill?
faq.answer: If you realize you've skipped the skill check, you can invoke it retroactively. The red flags in this skill help you recognize when you've been rationalizing.
